title: practical english usage - Part 333
content:
She's good at scientific subjects, like mathematics, (     ...-as-mathematics.) We can use like to give examples.

He's very like his father. She looks a bit like Queen Victoria.

We   can use very, quite and other adverbs of degree before like.

Like his parents, he is a vegetarian.

He ran like the wind, (     ... as the wind.) not My sister looks like me. (     . . ,-as-me/) like + noun/pronoun about similarity.

Like can be a preposition. We   use like, not as, before a noun or pronoun to talk 1  like    (similarity):     like    me We   can also use as to talk about function - the jobs that people or things do.

We   can use like or as to say that things are similar.

515  like    and   as:  similarity,      function You can sit wherever you like.

Do it any way you like. Come when you like.

'Can I go now? 'If you like.' (     If you like to.) subordinate clauses. Note that to is not used.

When  we offer people a choice, we  often use like to mean 'want (to)’  in 7 if  you     like,    etc Word  Problems from A  to Z     Section 31 «516 vocabulary’            likely I think she'll very/most likely be late.

Note also the informal adverb phrases very/most likely.

Snow is very unlikely.

l'he opposite is unlikely.

I don't think a Labour victory is likely. What's a likely date for the election?

Likely is an adjective with a similar meaning to probable.

For comparisons with as . .. as, ►          For alike,             For like used instead of as if, Note that as is often pronounced /əz/   (►  31.3).

he and ɪ  have similar attitudes.) Like your brother, I must warn you to be careful, (ɪ am not your brother, but As your brother, I must warn you to be careful, (ɪ am your brother.) Compare this use of as with like.

A crocodile starts life as an egg.

Please don't use that knife as a screwdriver.

lie worked as a waiter for two years, (     ... like a waiter.) belong to, etc. In this case, as is a preposition, used before a noun.

what jobs people do, what purposes things are used for, what category they Another use of as is to say what function or role a person or thing has - He  worked         as   a  waiter.

7 function      or  role: Like James, I am not a Conservative. (James is not a Conservative.) Like Emily, I don't smoke. (Emily doesn’ t smoke.) Before a negative clause, the comparison refers to the whole clause.

/ am not a Conservative, like Joe. (Joe is a Conservative.) I don't smoke, like Megan. (Megan smokes.) the positive part of what comes before.

After a negative clause, a comparison with as or like usually refers only to 6 comparison         with    as  and   like    after    negatives not 1 am sending you the bill, as was agreed, (     .. . as it was-agreed;) . •  •  J not   s As is well known, more people get colds in wet weather, (     A  it is well expressions (►  256).

as was agreed. Note that there is no subject it after as in these There are some passive expressions of this kind - for example as is well known; I am sending you the bill for the repairs, as we agreed.

As you know, next Tuesday's meeting has been cancelled.

Examples are as you know, as we agreed, as you suggested.

'common ground’  - known to both speaker/writer and listener/reader.

Some expressions beginning with as are used to introduce facts which  are 5 as  you     know,      etc vocabulary' •  517  long and (for) a long time Long ago, in a distant country, there lived a beautiful princess, (rather formal) Long after the accident he used to dream that he was dying.

We used to live in Paris, but that was long before you were born.

Long is also used in affirmative clauses to modify adverbs and conjunctions.

She sits dreaming all day long, (also all night/week/year long) Sorry 1 took so long. I'll be back before long.
